text: The surgeon mobilizes the omentum and mesentery of the sigmoid and left colon for exposure. Using hook electrocautery, the mesentery along the left colon is further mobilized. The left ureter and gonadal vessels are identified and protected with a gentle sweeping motion posteriorly.

The surgeon exposes and identifies the inferior mesenteric artery, follows the superior hemorrhoidal artery, and ligates it using hemoclips and ligature. Dissection of the rectosigmoid is continued from medial to lateral direction.

The surgeon elevates the rectum anteriorly and releases the posterior attachments to the mesorectal fascia using hook electrocautery. Subsequently, the peritoneal layer along the anterior rectum is released, and the rectum is separated from the rectovaginal septum.
The surgeon dissects the peritoneum along the anterior rectum, fully mobilizing the rectum by detaching it from its attachments to the pelvic floor.
The surgeon uses linear cutting staplers to transect the fully mobilized rectosigmoid, and the specimen is removed through a fan and seal incision.
The surgeon re-identifies the left ureter and the pre-sacral plexus before proceeding with the ligation of the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon secures the anvil to the distal portion of the left colon at the extraction site. Bowel continuity is re-established using circular staples in a tension-free manner. The integrity of the anastomosis is confirmed with a water seal test, followed by flexible sigmoidoscopy.
The surgeon performs suture rectopexy, securing the rectum to the sacral promontory using sutures.
The surgeon measures the pouch of Douglas using a ruler and places a biologic mesh measuring seven by three centimeters to obliterate the pouch. The mesh is then sutured securely in place.
